name: Enterprise Security Architecture Design
description: Designing security architectures for complex enterprise environments including on-premises, cloud, and hybrid
key_facts: SABSA (Sherwood Applied Business Security Architecture) provides a business-driven security framework
TOGAF provides enterprise architecture methodology that integrates security requirements
Security architecture must align with business objectives and risk tolerance
Defense in depth implements overlapping controls at network, host, application, and data layers
Architecture review boards evaluate security implications of design decisions
common_misconceptions: Security architecture is purely a technical discipline (it requires alignment with business strategy and risk management)
One security framework fits all organizations (framework selection depends on industry, size, and regulatory requirements)

name: Advanced Network Security Design
description: Designing secure network architectures with segmentation, zero trust, and software-defined networking
key_facts: Microsegmentation provides granular network isolation at the workload level
Software-Defined Networking (SDN) separates the control plane from the data plane for centralized management
SASE (Secure Access Service Edge) converges networking and security services in a cloud-delivered model
Zero trust network access (ZTNA) replaces traditional VPN with identity-based access
Network detection and response (NDR) uses AI/ML to identify threats in network traffic
common_misconceptions: VPN provides sufficient remote access security (ZTNA provides more granular, identity-based access control)
SDN eliminates the need for traditional security controls (SDN changes how controls are deployed, not whether they are needed)

name: Cloud and Hybrid Security Architecture
description: Securing multi-cloud and hybrid environments with appropriate controls and governance
key_facts: CASB (Cloud Access Security Broker) provides visibility and control over cloud service usage
CSPM (Cloud Security Posture Management) continuously monitors cloud configurations for compliance
Cloud workload protection platforms (CWPP) secure workloads across VMs, containers, and serverless
Multi-cloud security requires consistent policy enforcement across different providers
Shared responsibility model varies between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS
common_misconceptions: Cloud providers handle all security (the shared responsibility model makes customers responsible for their data and configurations)
Multi-cloud automatically improves security (it increases complexity and attack surface without proper governance)

name: Advanced Cryptographic Engineering
description: Implementing and managing cryptographic solutions including PKI, key management, and emerging algorithms
key_facts: Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC) provides equivalent security to RSA with smaller key sizes
Post-quantum cryptography addresses threats from quantum computers to current encryption
HSM (Hardware Security Module) provides tamper-resistant key storage and cryptographic operations
Certificate pinning prevents man-in-the-middle attacks by validating specific certificate identities
Key escrow allows authorized third parties to access encryption keys under defined conditions
common_misconceptions: AES-256 is unbreakable (side-channel attacks and implementation flaws can compromise even strong algorithms)
Quantum computing will immediately break all encryption (only asymmetric algorithms like RSA and ECC are vulnerable; AES remains secure with larger keys)

name: Secure Software Development and Integration
description: Integrating security into the software development lifecycle and DevSecOps practices
key_facts: DevSecOps integrates security into every phase of the CI/CD pipeline
Threat modeling (STRIDE, PASTA, DREAD) identifies potential threats during the design phase
SAST analyzes source code; DAST tests running applications; SCA identifies vulnerable dependencies
Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) inventories all components and dependencies for supply chain security
Secure coding standards (OWASP, CERT) provide guidelines for preventing common vulnerabilities
common_misconceptions: Security testing at the end of development is sufficient (shift-left means integrating security from the design phase)
Open source dependencies are always safe (they can contain known vulnerabilities requiring regular scanning)

name: Advanced Identity and Access Engineering
description: Implementing enterprise identity solutions including federation, privileged access, and identity governance
key_facts: Identity federation enables SSO across organizational boundaries using SAML, OAuth 2.0, and OIDC
Privileged Access Management (PAM) secures, monitors, and audits privileged account usage
Just-in-time (JIT) access provides temporary elevated privileges only when needed
Identity governance automates access reviews, certification campaigns, and lifecycle management
Conditional access policies enforce MFA, device compliance, and location-based restrictions
common_misconceptions: SSO reduces security because it creates a single point of failure (SSO with MFA actually improves security by reducing password fatigue)
PAM is only for IT administrators (any account with elevated privileges requires PAM controls)

name: Threat Hunting and Advanced Detection
description: Proactive threat hunting methodologies, hypothesis-driven analysis, and advanced detection techniques
key_facts: Threat hunting proactively searches for threats that evade automated detection
Hypothesis-driven hunting starts with a theory based on threat intelligence or behavioral anomalies
MITRE ATT&CK provides a knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques for threat hunting
Indicators of Compromise (IoCs) vs Indicators of Attack (IoAs): IoCs are post-breach evidence; IoAs detect active attacks
User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA) uses machine learning to detect anomalous behavior
common_misconceptions: SIEM alone is sufficient for threat detection (threat hunting finds threats that SIEM rules miss)
Threat hunting requires advanced AI tools (structured hunting with hypothesis and ATT&CK framework is effective)

name: Advanced Incident Response and Recovery
description: Enterprise incident response for advanced threats, forensics at scale, and business continuity
key_facts: Advanced Persistent Threats (APTs) require extended response timelines and thorough eradication
Memory forensics reveals fileless malware, injected processes, and command-and-control activity
DFIR (Digital Forensics and Incident Response) combines forensic analysis with active response
Business continuity planning ensures critical operations continue during and after incidents
Tabletop exercises and red team/blue team exercises test and improve IR capabilities
common_misconceptions: Incident response is only reactive (proactive threat hunting and preparation are critical components)
Re-imaging all systems eliminates all threats (APTs may have persistence mechanisms across the environment)

name: Enterprise Risk Management Frameworks
description: Risk management frameworks, quantitative and qualitative analysis, and risk treatment at enterprise scale
key_facts: NIST RMF provides a structured process: Categorize, Select, Implement, Assess, Authorize, Monitor
ISO 27005 provides guidelines for information security risk management
Quantitative analysis uses financial metrics: ALE = SLE x ARO; SLE = AV x EF
Risk appetite defines the level of risk an organization is willing to accept
Third-party risk management evaluates vendor security through questionnaires, audits, and SOC reports
common_misconceptions: Risk can be completely eliminated (risk can only be reduced, transferred, avoided, or accepted)
Third-party vendors inheriting customer data share the same risk tolerance (vendor risk must be independently assessed)

name: Regulatory Compliance and Security Policies
description: Enterprise compliance programs, policy frameworks, and governance structures
key_facts: Data sovereignty laws require data to be stored and processed within specific jurisdictions
Privacy regulations: GDPR (EU), CCPA (California), PIPEDA (Canada), LGPD (Brazil)
Security policy hierarchy: policies (mandatory requirements), standards (specific implementations), procedures (step-by-step), guidelines (recommendations)
Board-level reporting translates security metrics into business risk and financial impact
Audit management includes internal audits, external audits, and continuous compliance monitoring
common_misconceptions: Compliance guarantees security (compliance is a minimum baseline; security requires continuous improvement beyond compliance)
Security policies only apply to the IT department (security policies apply to all employees and stakeholders)
